topic: Using Adverbs and Register in Dialogue
title: Making Characters Speak: Adverbs and Register in Action
explanation_chunks: Remember how 'register' helps characters sound real? Now we're going to dive deeper into how 'adverbs' and 'adverbial phrases' are super important for showing a character's register and feelings.
When a character speaks 'somewhat irritably,' you can almost hear their grumpy tone! These words tell us *how* they are saying something, not just *what* they are saying.

In the story of Eager and the washing machine, notice how the machine asks many questions. This shows its 'officious' register – trying to be helpful but also a bit bossy and formal, because it's a machine following instructions.
By carefully choosing adverbs like 'decisively' or an adverbial phrase like 'in an alarmed voice', you can really bring your dialogue to life and make each character sound unique.
examples: When Grumps the robot butler shouts, 'WHAT ARE YOU DOING?' his voice 'thunders.' This adverb tells us he's very angry and formal in his scolding.
If a character speaks 'angrily' or 'in a whisper,' you can imagine their voice and body language immediately.
key_points: Adverbs and adverbial phrases are powerful tools to show character emotions and how they speak.
Different characters will use different registers, which is shown through their vocabulary and how they express themselves.
Practise creating and using a wide range of adverbs and adverbial phrases to make your dialogue vivid.
Always remember correct punctuation and layout for dialogue, starting a new line each time a different character speaks.

vocabulary.definition: Like someone who acts like an expert and is helpful, but also a bit bossy or formal, especially about rules.
A chemical liquid or powder used for washing and cleaning.
A conversation between two or more characters in a story.

explain_again: Imagine your voice is a magic paintbrush! Adverbs are like adding bright colours to your character's words – saying 'shouted angrily' is much more colourful than just 'shouted'. And changing your voice (register) is like painting a formal portrait for a king, or a silly cartoon for your friend! It makes the story more fun to read.
fun_analogy: Adverbs are like sound effects for your dialogue! Instead of just 'He said,' adding an adverb like 'He whispered' or 'He exclaimed loudly' gives the reader a perfect sound effect in their head, making the conversation much more exciting, like a movie scene.
